content.text: A champion who completes onboarding knows what to do. A champion who has a well-designed toolkit knows

across every interaction, every week, without reinventing the wheel. The gap between those two states is what causes champions to deliver uneven support — excellent in their first enthusiastic weeks, increasingly improvised as the novelty fades.

The toolkit's job is to lower the cognitive load of being a champion. If running an office hours session requires a champion to prepare from scratch each time, they will gradually stop running sessions. If it requires picking a format from a card, filling in a template, and pressing play on a pre-built structure, they will run sessions reliably for 12 months.
This document describes eight toolkit components, how each is structured, and the Python models for validating and tracking the artefacts champions use and produce.
Each component addresses a specific champion activity. Together they form a complete support system that a new champion can use from day one and an experienced champion can extend over time.
A two-page reference covering everything a new champion needs in their first week. Not a training document — a reference card.
Charter signed, tool access confirmed, compliance module done, buddy contact added, artefact library bookmarked, intro message posted in team channel
Your role in three sentences: I help my team use GenAI tools more effectively. I share what I learn. I connect people to the right resource when a question is outside my scope.
Not a trainer, not a compliance officer, not an IT helpdesk, not a shadow AI team — know these boundaries and name them without apology
Post introduction, schedule shadow session with buddy, read three artefacts from the repository, identify one question you hear in your team that you could answer
Buddy name + contact, CoP lead name + contact, IT provisioning contact, compliance escalation contact

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class QuickStartChecklist:
    champion_name: str
    buddy_name: str
    # Day 1
    charter_signed: bool = False
    tool_access_confirmed: bool = False
    compliance_module_complete: bool = False
    buddy_contacted: bool = False
    artefact_library_bookmarked: bool = False
    team_intro_posted: bool = False
    # Week 1
    shadow_session_scheduled: bool = False
    artefacts_read: int = 0
    first_question_identified: Optional[str] = None

    def day_one_complete(self) -> bool:
        return all([
            self.charter_signed,
            self.tool_access_confirmed,
            self.compliance_module_complete,
            self.buddy_contacted,
            self.artefact_library_bookmarked,
            self.team_intro_posted,
        ])

    def week_one_complete(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.day_one_complete() and
            self.shadow_session_scheduled and
            self.artefacts_read >= 3 and
            self.first_question_identified is not None
        )

    def status(self) -> str:
        if self.week_one_complete():
            return "READY_FOR_ONBOARDING_WEEK_2"
        if self.day_one_complete():
            return "DAY_ONE_DONE_WEEK_ONE_IN_PROGRESS"
        incomplete = [f for f in [
            "charter_signed", "tool_access_confirmed", "compliance_module_complete",
            "buddy_contacted", "artefact_library_bookmarked", "team_intro_posted"
        ] if not getattr(self, f)]
        return f"DAY_ONE_INCOMPLETE: {', '.join(incomplete)}"

checklist = QuickStartChecklist(
    champion_name="Jordan Lee", buddy_name="Sam Rivera",
    charter_signed=True, tool_access_confirmed=True,
    compliance_module_complete=True, buddy_contacted=True,
    artefact_library_bookmarked=True, team_intro_posted=False,
)
print(checklist.status())
# DAY_ONE_INCOMPLETE: team_intro_posted
Office hours are the highest-visibility champion activity. Inconsistent facilitation quality erodes attendance within two months. The playbook standardises the format without scripting it.
Name the theme if there is one; remind attendees of the 'ask anything, we figure it out together' norm; one safety statement
Champion demos one technique, prompt pattern, or recent artefact from the repository — prepared in advance
Questions from attendees; champion can say 'I don't know, let me find out' — model intellectual honesty
Champion names one thing each questioner can try before the next session
Announce next session date; remind attendees how to submit questions asynchronously

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class OfficeHoursSession:
    session_date: date
    champion_name: str
    theme: Optional[str]        # None = open Q&A
    attendee_count: int
    questions_handled: int
    questions_deferred: int     # "I'll find out and post in the channel"
    spotlight_topic: str
    action_items_assigned: int
    notes_published: bool       # posted to artefact repository within 24h

    def effectiveness_score(self) -> float:
        """0-100 score based on structural completeness."""
        score = 0.0
        if self.attendee_count >= 3:          score += 20
        if self.questions_handled >= 2:       score += 20
        if self.action_items_assigned >= 1:   score += 20
        if self.notes_published:              score += 25
        # Deferral rate: deferring < 30% of questions is healthy (shows real breadth)
        total_q = self.questions_handled + self.questions_deferred
        if total_q > 0:
            deferral_rate = self.questions_deferred / total_q
            if deferral_rate <= 0.30:
                score += 15
        return score

    def feedback(self) -> list[str]:
        fb = []
        if self.attendee_count < 3:
            fb.append("Low attendance — promote next session 3 days in advance via team channel")
        if not self.notes_published:
            fb.append("Notes not published — post a summary within 24h to extend session value")
        if self.action_items_assigned == 0:
            fb.append("No action items assigned — end every session with at least one concrete next step per attendee")
        return fb

session = OfficeHoursSession(
    session_date=date(2025, 4, 8),
    champion_name="Jordan Lee",
    theme="Prompt chaining for multi-step tasks",
    attendee_count=7,
    questions_handled=5,
    questions_deferred=1,
    spotlight_topic="Using Copilot to chain a data extraction prompt into a summary prompt",
    action_items_assigned=4,
    notes_published=False,
)
print(f"Score: {session.effectiveness_score()}/100")
print("Feedback:", session.feedback())
# Score: 75.0/100
# Feedback: ['Notes not published -- post a summary within 24h to extend session value']

The knowledge repository lives or dies by the quality of what is submitted. The contribution kit provides a submission template and a peer-review checklist so champions produce repository-ready artefacts, not drafts that require heavy editing.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class PromptTemplate:
    title: str
    role_context: str           # Who is this prompt for?
    use_case: str               # What task does it accomplish?
    prompt_text: str            # The actual prompt, with [PLACEHOLDERS] in brackets
    example_input: str          # A concrete example of filled-in placeholders
    example_output_excerpt: str # First 2-3 sentences of representative output
    known_limitations: list[str]  # Must be non-empty — honesty is mandatory
    approved_tools: list[str]   # Which approved tools this works on
    contributor: str
    peer_reviewed_by: Optional[str] = None

    def is_submission_ready(self) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
        issues = []
        if not self.prompt_text.strip():
            issues.append("Prompt text is empty")
        if "[" not in self.prompt_text:
            issues.append("No placeholders found — use [PLACEHOLDER] syntax for variable parts")
        if not self.example_input.strip():
            issues.append("Example input is required")
        if not self.example_output_excerpt.strip():
            issues.append("Example output excerpt is required")
        if not self.known_limitations:
            issues.append("known_limitations must not be empty — document at least one limitation")
        if not self.approved_tools:
            issues.append("Specify which approved tools this prompt works with")
        return len(issues) == 0, issues

    def peer_review_checklist(self) -> list[str]:
        """Items the peer reviewer should verify."""
        return [
            "Prompt runs as-is on at least one listed approved tool",
            "Example output excerpt is genuine (not cherry-picked ideal output)",
            "At least one known limitation is accurate and non-trivial",
            "No confidential data in example_input or example_output_excerpt",
            "Title clearly describes the use case (findable by search)",
        ]

template = PromptTemplate(
    title="Variance Commentary Draft — Finance",
    role_context="Finance analyst or controller preparing monthly variance reports",
    use_case="Draft a plain-English explanation of a budget variance given the numbers",
    prompt_text=(
        "You are a finance analyst. Write a 3-paragraph variance commentary for [REPORTING_PERIOD]. "
        "Actual [ACTUAL_METRIC]: [ACTUAL_VALUE]. Budget: [BUDGET_VALUE]. "
        "Variance: [VARIANCE_VALUE] ([VARIANCE_PCT]%). "
        "Key driver: [DRIVER_DESCRIPTION]. Tone: professional, factual, no jargon. "
        "Do not invent additional context not provided."
    ),
    example_input="REPORTING_PERIOD=March 2025; ACTUAL_METRIC=Operating Expense; ACTUAL_VALUE=4.2M; BUDGET_VALUE=3.9M; VARIANCE_VALUE=+0.3M; VARIANCE_PCT=+7.7%; DRIVER_DESCRIPTION=unplanned contractor spend in Q1 project ramp",
    example_output_excerpt="Operating expense for March 2025 came in at 4.2M against a budget of 3.9M, a variance of +0.3M (+7.7%). The primary driver was unplanned contractor spend associated with the Q1 project ramp...",
    known_limitations=[
        "Output must be reviewed by the report owner before sending — model may misinterpret driver descriptions",
        "Does not account for prior-period context; include YTD data manually if needed",
    ],
    approved_tools=["Microsoft Copilot for M365", "Approved ChatGPT Enterprise workspace"],
    contributor="Jordan Lee",
)
ready, issues = template.is_submission_ready()
print(f"Ready: {ready}")  # True
print("Review checklist:", template.peer_review_checklist())
The escalation guide is a single-page reference that a champion keeps open during office hours. Its value is in
so champions can decline out-of-scope questions without apologising or hedging.
How to write clearer prompts; how to reduce hallucination risk; prompt templates for common tasks
Legal review of AI-generated output; compliance sign-off on AI-assisted decisions
Which approved tools to use for which tasks; how to access tools; basic troubleshooting
Data classification questions; requests to use non-approved tools; new tool procurement
How to evaluate and improve AI outputs; when to verify; how to cite AI-assisted work
Whether a specific output is legally or regulatorily compliant
Whether a use case sounds feasible; how to describe it for the pipeline; who to contact
Business case approval; budget allocation; build vs buy decisions

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

EscalationCategory = Literal[
    "COMPLIANCE", "DATA_CLASSIFICATION", "NON_APPROVED_TOOL",
    "LEGAL_REVIEW", "BUDGET", "IT_PROVISIONING", "UNKNOWN"
]

@dataclass
class EscalationRegister:
    compliance_contact: str
    data_governance_contact: str
    it_provisioning_contact: str
    legal_ai_contact: str
    cop_lead: str

    def route(self, question_category: EscalationCategory) -> str:
        routing = {
            "COMPLIANCE":          self.compliance_contact,
            "DATA_CLASSIFICATION": self.data_governance_contact,
            "NON_APPROVED_TOOL":   self.it_provisioning_contact,
            "LEGAL_REVIEW":        self.legal_ai_contact,
            "BUDGET":              self.cop_lead,
            "IT_PROVISIONING":     self.it_provisioning_contact,
            "UNKNOWN":             self.cop_lead,
        }
        contact = routing[question_category]
        return (
            f"This question is outside my advisory scope as a champion. "
            f"I'll refer you to {contact}, who handles this area. "
            f"I can help you frame the question if that would be useful."
        )

register = EscalationRegister(
    compliance_contact="AI Compliance Team (ai-compliance@company.com)",
    data_governance_contact="Data Governance Office (ext. 4422)",
    it_provisioning_contact="IT Service Desk (servicedesk@company.com)",
    legal_ai_contact="Legal AI Counsel (legal-ai@company.com)",
    cop_lead="Sam Rivera (sam.rivera@company.com)",
)
print(register.route("DATA_CLASSIFICATION"))
# This question is outside my advisory scope as a champion. I'll refer you to
# Data Governance Office (ext. 4422), who handles this area.
# I can help you frame the question if that would be useful.
Champions who want to deliver a team workshop should not build slides from scratch. The workshop-in-a-box provides three pre-built 60-minute formats:
Teams with no prior GenAI experience
Understand what GenAI is, what it is not, and which tools are approved
Slides (20), facilitator notes, myth-busting card, tool access reference
Teams beginning to use approved tools
Write better prompts using the ROLE-TASK-FORMAT-CONSTRAINT structure; hands-on practice
Slides (15), 5 live exercises, prompt template starter pack
Identify and prioritise 3-5 candidate use cases from the team's workflows
Slides (12), use case canvas template, pipeline submission guide

Champions adapt the materials for their audience but do not rebuild from scratch. The adaptation time for a typical team should be under 90 minutes. The facilitator notes include timing guidance and likely audience questions with suggested responses.
Component 6: FAQ and Edge Case Bank

Champions encounter the same questions repeatedly — questions about data privacy, about whether AI-generated content needs disclosure, about what to do when an output is wrong. The FAQ bank collects curated answers so every champion gives a consistent, accurate response.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class FAQEntry:
    question: str
    answer: str
    category: str               # e.g. "data_privacy", "output_quality", "tool_access"
    approved_by: str            # compliance/legal reviewer who signed off on the answer
    approved_date: date
    last_reviewed: date

    def is_stale(self, as_of: date, staleness_days: int = 180) -> bool:
        return (as_of - self.last_reviewed).days > staleness_days

    def champion_response_script(self) -> str:
        return f"Great question. {self.answer} If you want the full policy detail, I can point you to the source."

entry = FAQEntry(
    question="Do I need to disclose in a document that I used AI to help write it?",
    answer=(
        "For internal documents, our current policy requires a disclosure footer "
        "if AI generated more than 30% of the text. The standard footer is: "
        "'This document was prepared with AI assistance and reviewed by the author.' "
        "For external documents (client-facing, regulatory), always use the disclosure "
        "regardless of contribution percentage and have your manager review before sending."
    ),
    category="output_quality",
    approved_by="Legal AI Counsel",
    approved_date=date(2025, 1, 15),
    last_reviewed=date(2025, 4, 1),
)
print(entry.champion_response_script())
print("Stale?", entry.is_stale(date(2025, 10, 1)))  # True (> 180 days)

Every FAQ entry is approved by a named reviewer with a date. Champions are instructed to check whether an entry is stale before quoting it in a high-stakes conversation — policy changes faster than the bank can be updated, so the review date matters.
Champions track their own activity monthly via a structured self-report. The self-report populates the CoP metrics pipeline and gives the champion a record of their contribution over time.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ChampionMonthlyReport:
    champion_name: str
    period: str                         # e.g. "2025-04"
    # Activity
    office_hours_sessions_run: int
    total_attendees: int
    async_questions_answered: int       # Slack/Teams questions handled outside sessions
    artefacts_contributed: int
    team_workshops_delivered: int
    use_cases_shepherded: int
    # Self-assessment
    capacity_adequate: bool             # Did 10% time hold?
    escalation_path_used: bool          # Did you escalate at least one question?
    top_question_this_month: str        # Most common question from peers
    one_thing_that_worked: str
    one_thing_that_didnt: str

    def activity_score(self) -> float:
        """Simple 0-100 indicator of activity level."""
        score = 0.0
        score += min(self.office_hours_sessions_run * 25, 50)   # max 2 sessions = 50pts
        score += min(self.artefacts_contributed * 20, 30)       # max 1.5 artefacts = 30pts
        score += min(self.async_questions_answered * 2, 20)     # max 10 Qs = 20pts
        return min(score, 100)

    def highlights(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "period": self.period,
            "score": self.activity_score(),
            "sessions": self.office_hours_sessions_run,
            "reach": self.total_attendees + self.async_questions_answered,
            "top_question": self.top_question_this_month,
        }

report = ChampionMonthlyReport(
    champion_name="Jordan Lee", period="2025-04",
    office_hours_sessions_run=2, total_attendees=12,
    async_questions_answered=8, artefacts_contributed=1,
    team_workshops_delivered=0, use_cases_shepherded=1,
    capacity_adequate=True, escalation_path_used=True,
    top_question_this_month="Can I use Copilot to summarise a client email before responding?",
    one_thing_that_worked="Posting the office hours summary in the team channel doubled engagement",
    one_thing_that_didnt="Tried an open-ended 'bring any question' format — too unstructured, attendance dropped",
)
print(report.highlights())
# {'period': '2025-04', 'score': 86.0, 'sessions': 2, 'reach': 20, 'top_question': 'Can I use Copilot...'}
Buddies are the primary support mechanism for new champions. The buddy guide covers pairing criteria, the 30-day check-in structure, and when to escalate to the CoP lead.

Pairing criteria: buddy and new champion should be in different BUs (cross-functional exposure), have been active for different durations (buddy has at least 3 months' experience), and have complementary roles (a technical champion buddied with a business-function champion improves both).

30-day check-in agenda: (1) What is going well? (2) What question have you been unable to answer? (3) Have you published at least one contribution? (4) Is your capacity holding? (5) What will you try differently next month?

Escalation trigger: if a new champion misses two consecutive office hours sessions, has not published any contribution by day 21, or reports capacity is already squeezed, the buddy escalates immediately to the CoP lead — do not wait for the 30-day gate.
A toolkit that becomes outdated is worse than no toolkit — champions confidently deliver wrong information. Establish a maintenance rhythm:
Any change to approved tools list or compliance requirements
Consistent negative feedback pattern in effectiveness scores
Submission quality declining (evidenced by peer-review rejection rate > 20%)
Any policy change affecting in-scope / out-of-scope boundary
New approved tool requiring its own workshop module
Any FAQ marked stale or found to be incorrect
Buddy programme consistently failing to prevent early dropout
The Champion Enablement Toolkit is eight components:
(monthly self-report populating the CoP pipeline), and

The toolkit reduces the per-session preparation burden to near zero for routine activities and provides consistent escalation behaviour across a champion network of any size. Maintain it on a defined rhythm — stale toolkit components erode the programme faster than no toolkit at all.
